exercises.title: Simulate argparse Argument Parsing
exercises.description: Simulate what argparse does by parsing a sys.argv-style list of strings. Build a simple argument parser that handles positional and optional arguments with defaults.
exercises.starterCode:
# Since argparse works with sys.argv (CLI), we simulate it
# by calling parse_args() with a custom list
import argparse

# Build the parser
parser = argparse.ArgumentParser(
    description="Text processing tool"
)

# TODO: Add positional argument 'filename' (str)
# TODO: Add optional argument '--lines' / '-n' (int, default=10)
# TODO: Add optional argument '--reverse' / '-r' (action='store_true')
# TODO: Add optional argument '--output' / '-o' (str, default=None)

# Test with different argument combinations
test_cases = [
    ["data.txt"],
    ["data.txt", "--lines", "20"],
    ["data.txt", "-n", "5", "--reverse"],
    ["report.csv", "-n", "3", "-r", "-o", "out.txt"],
]

for args_list in test_cases:
    args = parser.parse_args(args_list)
    print(f"file={args.filename}, lines={args.lines}, reverse={args.reverse}, output={args.output}")
exercises.expectedOutput:
file=data.txt, lines=10, reverse=False, output=None
file=data.txt, lines=20, reverse=False, output=None
file=data.txt, lines=5, reverse=True, output=None
file=report.csv, lines=3, reverse=True, output=out.txt

exercises.title: Subcommands with argparse
exercises.description: Build a CLI with subcommands (like git: git commit, git status). Implement 'create', 'list', and 'delete' subcommands that operate on an in-memory task list.
exercises.starterCode:
import argparse

# In-memory task store
tasks = []
next_id = [1]  # Use list to allow mutation in nested functions

def cmd_create(args):
    task = {"id": next_id[0], "title": args.title, "priority": args.priority}
    tasks.append(task)
    next_id[0] += 1
    print(f"Created task #{task['id']}: {args.title} (priority: {args.priority})")

def cmd_list(args):
    if not tasks:
        print("No tasks.")
        return
    filtered = tasks if not args.priority else [t for t in tasks if t['priority'] == args.priority]
    for t in filtered:
        print(f"  #{t['id']} [{t['priority']}] {t['title']}")

def cmd_delete(args):
    task = next((t for t in tasks if t['id'] == args.id), None)
    if task:
        tasks.remove(task)
        print(f"Deleted task #{args.id}")
    else:
        print(f"Task #{args.id} not found")


# Build parser with subcommands
parser = argparse.ArgumentParser(description="Task manager")
subparsers = parser.add_subparsers(dest="command")

# TODO: Add 'create' subcommand with:
#   positional 'title'
#   optional '--priority' / '-p' (default='medium', choices=['low', 'medium', 'high'])
#   set_defaults(func=cmd_create)

# TODO: Add 'list' subcommand with:
#   optional '--priority' / '-p' (default=None)
#   set_defaults(func=cmd_list)

# TODO: Add 'delete' subcommand with:
#   positional 'id' (type=int)
#   set_defaults(func=cmd_delete)


# Simulate CLI usage
commands = [
    ["create", "Buy groceries"],
    ["create", "Write report", "-p", "high"],
    ["create", "Read book", "-p", "low"],
    ["list"],
    ["list", "-p", "high"],
    ["delete", "2"],
    ["list"],
]

for cmd in commands:
    args = parser.parse_args(cmd)
    args.func(args)
exercises.expectedOutput:
Created task #1: Buy groceries (priority: medium)
Created task #2: Write report (priority: high)
Created task #3: Read book (priority: low)
  #1 [medium] Buy groceries
  #2 [high] Write report
  #3 [low] Read book
  #2 [high] Write report
Deleted task #2
  #1 [medium] Buy groceries
  #3 [low] Read book

exercises.title: Build a Mini CLI Tool: Text Processor
exercises.description: Implement a complete text processing CLI tool using argparse. The tool processes text data (from a string instead of a file) based on CLI arguments.
exercises.starterCode:
import argparse

# Sample text data (simulates reading from a file)
SAMPLE_TEXT = """Python is a versatile programming language.
It is used for web development, data science, and automation.
Python has a clean syntax and is easy to learn.
Many companies use Python for their backend services.
The Python community is large and supportive.
Python supports multiple programming paradigms."""

def process_text(text, args):
    lines = text.splitlines()

    # TODO: If args.grep is set, filter lines containing args.grep (case-insensitive if args.ignore_case)

    # TODO: If args.count, print "Lines: N" and return

    # TODO: If args.reverse, reverse the lines list

    # TODO: Apply args.head (take first N lines) if set

    # TODO: If args.number, prepend line numbers: "1: Python is..."

    # TODO: Print each line
    pass


parser = argparse.ArgumentParser(description="Text processor")
parser.add_argument("--grep", "-g", help="Filter lines containing this text")
parser.add_argument("--count", "-c", action="store_true", help="Count matching lines")
parser.add_argument("--reverse", "-r", action="store_true", help="Reverse line order")
parser.add_argument("--head", "-n", type=int, help="Show first N lines")
parser.add_argument("--number", action="store_true", help="Show line numbers")
parser.add_argument("--ignore-case", "-i", action="store_true", help="Case-insensitive grep")

# Test 1: grep for Python
print("--- grep Python ---")
process_text(SAMPLE_TEXT, parser.parse_args(["--grep", "Python"]))

# Test 2: count lines with 'Python'
print("--- count ---")
process_text(SAMPLE_TEXT, parser.parse_args(["--grep", "Python", "--count"]))

# Test 3: head 3 with numbers
print("--- head 3 numbered ---")
process_text(SAMPLE_TEXT, parser.parse_args(["--head", "3", "--number"]))
exercises.expectedOutput:
--- grep Python ---
Python is a versatile programming language.
Python has a clean syntax and is easy to learn.
Many companies use Python for their backend services.
The Python community is large and supportive.
Python supports multiple programming paradigms.
--- count ---
Lines: 5
--- head 3 numbered ---
1: Python is a versatile programming language.
2: It is used for web development, data science, and automation.
3: Python has a clean syntax and is easy to learn.
hints: if args.grep: lines = [l for l in lines if args.grep.lower() in l.lower()] if args.ignore_case else [l for l in lines if args.grep in l]
if args.number: lines = [f'{i+1}: {l}' for i, l in enumerate(lines)]

warmup.title: Quick Recall: argparse Basics
warmup.description: What does this code print? Trace the argparse argument parsing.
warmup.starterCode:
import argparse

parser = argparse.ArgumentParser()
parser.add_argument('name')
parser.add_argument('--count', type=int, default=1)
parser.add_argument('--upper', action='store_true')

args = parser.parse_args(['Alice', '--count', '3'])
print(args.name)
print(args.count)
print(args.upper)
